title: The Site Master File: A Complete Guide
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A practical guide to the site master file: what EU GMP Part III requires, how to structure it to PIC/S, and how to keep it inspection-ready and current.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner viewed at a shallow angle across a polished dark surface in a calm pharmaceutical quality office. A slim closed document folder and a single architectural site-plan drawing rest in soft focus beside a sleek tablet, all bathed in cool teal and cyan light against a deep navy background, with one subtle green accent glowing from a screen edge. Soft natural light rakes gently from the left, producing delicate reflections and a shallow depth of field that dissolves the far background into smooth navy gradients. The mood is precise, authoritative and quietly premium, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, no readable documents.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a site master file and a quality manual?
faqs.a: A site master file describes a specific manufacturing or importation site: its activities, premises, equipment, personnel and the GMP arrangements in place. A quality manual describes the pharmaceutical quality system at a higher, organisational level. The SMF is site-specific and inspector-facing, structured to EU GMP Part III, whereas the quality manual frames the overall QMS; the two are complementary rather than interchangeable.

faqs.q: Who is responsible for maintaining the site master file?
faqs.a: Every holder of a Manufacturer's/Importer's Authorisation must maintain a site master file, and ownership normally sits with the Qualified Person or the head of quality. It must be managed as a controlled document with version control, formal approval and a defined review cycle. Updates should be driven through the change control process whenever a material change occurs on site, not left until an inspection is imminent.

faqs.q: How long should a site master file be?
faqs.a: The PIC/S Explanatory Notes adopted in EU GMP Part III indicate that the body of the document, excluding annexes, should not normally exceed around 25 to 30 pages. Detail such as the manufacturing authorisation, site plans, system schematics and lists of contractors belongs in the annexes. If the main text runs much longer, it is usually carrying procedural detail that should be referenced rather than reproduced.
